EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell east of New Hampton. The hail broke windows and damaged siding on several homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong upper level storm system swept across the northern Plains during the day on Sunday August 31st. Ahead of the storm system low pressure developed over the high Plains and allowed for warm and moist air to pool over the mid Missouri River Valley creating strong instability by mid to late afternoon. As a cold front and associated upper level jet streak interacted with the instability, thunderstorms developed from northeast Nebraska into Northern Kansas. These thunderstorms moved east through the rest of the afternoon and evening hours, crossing eastern Nebraska and western Iowa produced mainly hail and wind damage, but heavy rainfall also occurred in parts of the area as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swath of wind damage that began in far NE Wright County broadened and strengthened as it entered NW Franklin County. An EF1 tornado was embedded within this wind damage. Wind gusts of 70-80 mph were found in a 1 to 2 mile wide and 4 to 5 mile long corridor, with locally higher gusts over 100 mph. One house lost half of its roof and multiple outbuildings were destroyed. One hog confinement building disintegrated--its remnants scattered for over 2 miles to the south. Damage point is at the house that lost its roof. Hog building was 1.5 miles SE of this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the high Plains during the afternoon on Tuesday June 3rd, and then across the mid Missouri River Valley on Tuesday night. Ahead of the system, warm, moist and unstable air was pulled north as a warm front lifted into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa by mid afternoon. The combination of the strong vertical wind shear near the boundary, and the strong instability created a volatile atmosphere across the area. The first of several rounds of supercell thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska after 1 pm and tracked east southeast. This intense storm moved across northeast Nebraska, and into western and finally southern Iowa producing severe weather. Additional thunderstorms developed ahead and behind this initial wave, also producing severe weather. Additional supercells developed in central Nebraska and moved into southeast Nebraska during the evening hours. The severe weather ended after midnight across the area. Due to the repeated tracking of thunderstorms across the same area, flash flooding was observed in several areas which eventually led to river flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the Council Bluffs Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell across the upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June and led to major flooding along the Mississippi River and its tributaries that continued into early July.  Rainfall totals for the month of June ranged from 8.24 inches at the Davenport Municipal Airport to 13.29 inches at Cedar Rapids and 12.57 inches at Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains that fell across the Upper Midwest during the last two weeks of June brought the Mississippi River at Camanche above its major flood stage of 20.5 at approximately 825 PM and fell below 20.5 feet around 1225 PM on July 5. The river crested at 20.9 feet from noon on July 3 to 1100 PM July 4.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains that fell across the Upper Midwest during the last two weeks of June brought the Mississippi River at LeClaire above its major flood stage of 13.5 at approximately 200 PM and fell below major flood stage around 1225 PM on July 7. The river crested at 14.4 feet from 600 PM July 3 to 600 PM on July 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system moved from the northern plains into the upper Midwest during the evening and overnight hours of August 31 and September 1.  This led to the development of a line of thunderstorms that stretched from southern Minnesota  southward into eastern Kansas.  The line of storms slowly moved eastward, producing damaging winds near Independence, IA just before 11 pm CDT August 31.  Other thunderstorms went on to produce severe wind gusts and minor damage between midnight and 1 am CDT September 1 in Keokuk and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was recorded near Sigourney, IA at 1209 am CDT September 1/1109 pm CST August 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure moved northeast into the central U.S. during the day on the the 7th.  The atmosphere had deep moisture present with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 1.75 inch range across southern Iowa.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with modest CAPE available.  An MCS moved into the state as the upper low approached.  Widespread rainfall occurred with heavy rainfall across the south.  Three to five inches fell in parts of Adams, Appanoose and Davis Counties.  One vehicle became stranded in Davis County, requiring a rescue of the occupants.  Roads were washed out in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee measured 1.30 inches of rain through 1220 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level system was moving into south central Canada.  The upper level winds were increasing as a closed upper low settled into that area.  Warm air advection continued across Iowa through the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th.  The atmosphere was very unstable with CAPE values around 4000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1000 and 1500 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 800 J/kg range.  Strong shear was present, with an effective shear of 50 to 60 kts.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 15,000 feet.  Precipitable water increased to between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  There was a considerable amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere, between 100 and 175 J/kg.  That combined with an LCl of only 750 meters was a favorable environment for tornado development.  Thunderstorms formed rapidly in northwest and north central Iowa by the late afternoon and evening hours.  They spread and continued to move east through the night.  All modes of severe weather occurred with tornadoes occurring during the early and mid evening hours.  Two tornadoes were reported in the Stanhope area near the Webster and Hamilton County line.  A tornado touched down in Grundy County north of Conrad.  Winds caused damage in many areas across the north half of Iowa.  Hail was common with most of the storms in spite of the high freezing level.  Much of the hail an inch or less in diameter.  Larger hail of golf ball to tennis ball size fell in Butler and Bremer Counties.  A strong storm formed in Ringgold and Union Counties and dropped golf ball size hail.  The event produced very heavy rainfall in many locations.  The ground had become nearly saturated from previous rounds of storms during the week prior.  Flooding and flash flooding became a problem in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported that thunderstorm wind gusts blew down corn stalks.  Nickel to dime size hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across the region caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. This led to the development of severe thunderstorms across eastern Iowa north of Interstate 80. These thunderstorms produced strong wind gusts over 60 mph and torrential rains with 1 to 4 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down in rural countryside east of Stuart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees and large limbs were blown onto homes in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved into Tama county from Grundy county.  It remained in agricultural fields doing only crop damage.  This is the second and final segment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated, also very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure moved northeast into the central U.S. during the day on the the 7th.  The atmosphere had deep moisture present with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 1.75 inch range across southern Iowa.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with modest CAPE available.  An MCS moved into the state as the upper low approached.  Widespread rainfall occurred with heavy rainfall across the south.  Three to five inches fell in parts of Adams, Appanoose and Davis Counties.  One vehicle became stranded in Davis County, requiring a rescue of the occupants.  Roads were washed out in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports three inches of rain today.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets in the city were flooded.  Many others from Garner to Goodell also had flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided very early in the month on the Turkey River but continued until the middle of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late June on the Mississippi River in McGregor continued into the second week of July before the river went below the flood stage. The river crested over 2.5 feet above the flood stage at 18.68 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois the evening of August 4, as a weak cold front slowly pushed south over the region. One storm surpassed severe levels, producing quarter sized hail in Coralville, IA just before 7 pm.

Heavy rains fell across the upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June and led to major flooding along the Mississippi River and its tributaries that continued into early July.  Rainfall totals for the month of June ranged from 8.24 inches at the Davenport Municipal Airport to 13.29 inches at Cedar Rapids and 12.57 inches at Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across the Upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last 2 weeks of June caused the Iowa River to rise to its major flood stage of 25 feet around 6PM on July 1 and fall below major flood stage around 115AM on July 9. The river crested at 30.2 feet from 5AM to 6PM on July 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell across the upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June and led to major flooding along the Mississippi River and its tributaries that continued into early July.  Rainfall totals for the month of June ranged from 8.24 inches at the Davenport Municipal Airport to 13.29 inches at Cedar Rapids and 12.57 inches at Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across the Upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last 2 weeks of June caused the Maquoketa River at Maquoketa to rise above the major flood stage of 28.5 feet around 1245 AM on July 1 and fall below the major flood stage around 200 AM on July 2. The river crested at 30.6 feet from noon to 600 PM on July 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system moved from the northern plains into the upper Midwest during the evening and overnight hours of August 31 and September 1.  This led to the development of a line of thunderstorms that stretched from southern Minnesota  southward into eastern Kansas.  The line of storms slowly moved eastward, producing damaging winds near Independence, IA just before 11 pm CDT August 31.  Other thunderstorms went on to produce severe wind gusts and minor damage between midnight and 1 am CDT September 1 in Keokuk and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 60 mph blew down a two inch diameter tree branch in a neighbor's yard around 1245 am CDT September 1/1145 pm CST August 31. Some loose objects in the area were also blown around during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across the region caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. This led to the development of severe thunderstorms across eastern Iowa north of Interstate 80. These thunderstorms produced strong wind gusts over 60 mph and torrential rains with 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a two trees down in DeWitt along with several branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms tracked eastward across portions of Eastern Iowa, Northwest Illinois, and Northeast Missouri during the morning of September 15, as a warm front lifted north into Northern Missouri.  One storm was severe, when it produced quarter sized hail in Batavia, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Batavia, IA around 735 am CDT September 15.

Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville. By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated around 60 MPH damaged a HyVee sign.

During the afternoon of the 7th, a boundary extended west-northwest to east-southeast across southwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE of 3000 to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 J/kg, with 300 to 600 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was stronger than the day before, with about 50 kts of effective shear . CAPE in the 0-3 km layer was 25 to 75 J/kg.  The lifted index had fallen to -8 C.  Precipitable water increased to 1.6 to 1.8 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 14,500 feet.  Low level lapse rates were between 6 and 7 C with the LCL fairly high at 1250 meters.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in Nebraska where the cap weakened first.  They spread into southern Iowa by the evening hours.  As the line of storm intensified, a MARC signature was evident on the Radar.  Winds began to reach severe levels as the storms moved through the Creston area.  The strongest wind gusts were in the Lamoni area, where a 74 MPH wind gust was recorded.  Significant tree and power line damage was done across the city.  Governor Terry E. Branstad signed a letter to be delivered to President Obama requesting a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 Iowa counties where significant damage was sustained from severe weather that produced damaging winds, tornadoes, heavy rains, hail, and thunderstorms beginning June 26 through July 7, 2014.  The counties included in the request are: Audubon, Black Hawk, Butler, Cedar, Des Moines, Grundy, Hamilton, Hardin, Ida, Iowa, Jackson, Jasper, Johnson, Jones, Keokuk, Lee, Linn, Mahaska, Muscatine, Poweshiek, Tama and Washington.  President Obama has approved Governor Branstad���s request for a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 counties impacted by severe weather during the period of June 26-July 7.  The declaration will provide funding under the Public Assistance and Hazard Mitigation Programs.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell across the upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June and led to major flooding along the Mississippi River and its tributaries that continued into early July.  Rainfall totals for the month of June ranged from 8.24 inches at the Davenport Municipal Airport to 13.29 inches at Cedar Rapids and 12.57 inches at Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the Upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June caused the North Skunk River near Sigourney to rise above its major flood stage of 21 feet around 1100 AM on July 1 and fall below major flood stage around 930 AM on July 5. The river crested at 23 feet around 11PM on July 2 and remained at the crest through 100 AM on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell across the upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June and led to major flooding along the Mississippi River and its tributaries that continued into early July.  Rainfall totals for the month of June ranged from 8.24 inches at the Davenport Municipal Airport to 13.29 inches at Cedar Rapids and 12.57 inches at Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the Upper Midwest and eastern Iowa during the last two weeks of June caused the Cedar River at Cedar Rapids to rise above the major flood stage of 16 feet during the first week in July.  The first occurred between 100 PM on July 1 and 600 AM on July 2 with a crest of 16.4 feet at 600 PM on July 1. The second rise above the major flood stage occurred between noon on July 3 and 745 PM on July 4 with a crest of around 16.5 feet around 600 AM on July 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across the region caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. This led to the development of severe thunderstorms across eastern Iowa north of Interstate 80. These thunderstorms produced strong wind gusts over 60 mph and torrential rains with 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the general public reported uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.

The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County sheriff reported traffic sign damage and a metal shed roof removed with the shed leaning near 145th Trail and Highway 5.

During the afternoon of the 7th, a boundary extended west-northwest to east-southeast across southwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE of 3000 to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 J/kg, with 300 to 600 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was stronger than the day before, with about 50 kts of effective shear . CAPE in the 0-3 km layer was 25 to 75 J/kg.  The lifted index had fallen to -8 C.  Precipitable water increased to 1.6 to 1.8 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 14,500 feet.  Low level lapse rates were between 6 and 7 C with the LCL fairly high at 1250 meters.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in Nebraska where the cap weakened first.  They spread into southern Iowa by the evening hours.  As the line of storm intensified, a MARC signature was evident on the Radar.  Winds began to reach severe levels as the storms moved through the Creston area.  The strongest wind gusts were in the Lamoni area, where a 74 MPH wind gust was recorded.  Significant tree and power line damage was done across the city.  Governor Terry E. Branstad signed a letter to be delivered to President Obama requesting a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 Iowa counties where significant damage was sustained from severe weather that produced damaging winds, tornadoes, heavy rains, hail, and thunderstorms beginning June 26 through July 7, 2014.  The counties included in the request are: Audubon, Black Hawk, Butler, Cedar, Des Moines, Grundy, Hamilton, Hardin, Ida, Iowa, Jackson, Jasper, Johnson, Jones, Keokuk, Lee, Linn, Mahaska, Muscatine, Poweshiek, Tama and Washington.  President Obama has approved Governor Branstad���s request for a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 counties impacted by severe weather during the period of June 26-July 7.  The declaration will provide funding under the Public Assistance and Hazard Mitigation Programs.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were either blown down or uprooted on and near a golf course east of New Hampton. One of the downed trees caused one lane of State Highway 24 to be closed, a semi trailer was blown over onto its side and an outbuilding was destroyed. The cart shelter at the golf course was also damaged by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb. The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer. The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Narrow but strong area of wind damage in east central Bremer County. Worst damage was on a farmstead (point used for this event). A barn was flattened, multiple trees were snapped, and several empty grain bins were thrown into the trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down NW of Patterson where an RFD surge was located and a curling motion noted in the damage patterns just upstream of the start point. The tornado proceeded to damage several small groves of trees over the next 1.9 miles.  Here the tornado strengthened to EF1 where it snapped or uprooted several hundred trees. The path was often sporadic, owing likely to the hilly terrain of the area. The worst damage occurred to a home 2.5 miles NW of Bevington, which had the detached garage roof fail and take out part of the house roof as well. Roof and siding damage was noted to 5 homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne. The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a multiple segment tornado with three segments that passed through portions of Story, Marshall and Hardin counties.  This is the first segment in Story county.  This longer track tornado formed about a mile and a half east of Zearing where a farmstead sustained roof damage to multiple buildings. The relatively narrow tornado continued to the NE, with RFD winds destroying 6 grain bins and an outbuilding just south of the track. The tornado then passed into Marshall county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the last three hours was 3.25 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.50 inches in the past 30 minutes at the intersection of 73rd and 74th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 7th, a boundary extended west-northwest to east-southeast across southwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE of 3000 to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 J/kg, with 300 to 600 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was stronger than the day before, with about 50 kts of effective shear . CAPE in the 0-3 km layer was 25 to 75 J/kg.  The lifted index had fallen to -8 C.  Precipitable water increased to 1.6 to 1.8 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 14,500 feet.  Low level lapse rates were between 6 and 7 C with the LCL fairly high at 1250 meters.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in Nebraska where the cap weakened first.  They spread into southern Iowa by the evening hours.  As the line of storm intensified, a MARC signature was evident on the Radar.  Winds began to reach severe levels as the storms moved through the Creston area.  The strongest wind gusts were in the Lamoni area, where a 74 MPH wind gust was recorded.  Significant tree and power line damage was done across the city.  Governor Terry E. Branstad signed a letter to be delivered to President Obama requesting a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 Iowa counties where significant damage was sustained from severe weather that produced damaging winds, tornadoes, heavy rains, hail, and thunderstorms beginning June 26 through July 7, 2014.  The counties included in the request are: Audubon, Black Hawk, Butler, Cedar, Des Moines, Grundy, Hamilton, Hardin, Ida, Iowa, Jackson, Jasper, Johnson, Jones, Keokuk, Lee, Linn, Mahaska, Muscatine, Poweshiek, Tama and Washington.  President Obama has approved Governor Branstad���s request for a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 counties impacted by severe weather during the period of June 26-July 7.  The declaration will provide funding under the Public Assistance and Hazard Mitigation Programs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a power pole downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois the evening of August 4, as a weak cold front slowly pushed south over the region. One storm surpassed severe levels, producing quarter sized hail in Coralville, IA just before 7 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to dime sized hail fell about 2 miles south southwest of Iowa City, IA at 706 pm CDT Aug 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-severe hail up to the size of a dime was reported in Hills, IA during the morning of September 4.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a multiple segment tornado with three segments that passed through portions of Story, Marshall and Hardin counties.  This is the third and final segment in Hardin county.  The tornado cycled, made a hard right turn, and began to intensify again after crossing into Hardin County. EF1 damage was sustained to wooden power poles and trees along 335th St. between T and U Ave. Turning NE again after crossing U Ave, the tornado roamed through open farmland and weakened, only causing intermittent tree damage after crossing County Road S62 1.5 miles south of Union. The tornado dissipated shortly before reaching the Iowa River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a multiple segment tornado with three segments that passed through portions of Story, Marshall and Hardin counties.  This is the second segment in Marshall county.  After turning to the east and entering Marshall County, the tornado rapidly widened and intensified just after crossing County Highway S57. Severe damage occurred to a 500 yard wide stretch of trees along Minerva Creek along and north of 120th St. (part of the damage was likely attributable to RFD). The tornado continued east just north of 120th St. and completely destroyed a farm outbuilding, depositing the remnants of the building in the surrounding trees to the north and east. Very strong RFD winds caused widespread tree and building damage for over a mile south of the tornado path along this section of the track.  Turning northeast, the tornado tracked across open farmland for the next 2.5 miles, grazing one farmstead in the process. The narrowing tornado directly hit a farmstead south of the 105th St. and Fariman Ave. intersection, with mostly low EF1 tree damage sustained.  The tornado then continued into Hardin county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a multiple segment tornado with two segments that passed through portions of Marshall and Hardin counties.  This is the first segment in Marshall county.  As the first tornado from this supercell began to occlude and pull NE, a second tornado appears to have formed a mile to the south and east. A path of more intense damage was noted on a survey that began just north of Marble Road between Eastman and Fredrick Ave. The tornado grazed three farmsteads, causing mostly tree and some outbuilding damage. As the tornado moved into an area with more trees around mile three of its path, convergent wind patterns were noted on the ground survey to the north and south of the track, with a 100-150 yard wide path through a line of trees along a creek east of U Ave. After crossing Ingram Ave, the tornado caused roof damage to at least one outbuilding.  The tornado then moved in Hardin county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across northeast Iowa. A round of storms developed during the early morning hours of June 19th along a warm front and then a second round developed during the afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front. Radar estimates of 3 to 6 inches were common across southern Winneshiek and Allamakee Counties into the northern portions of Fayette and Clayton Counties. A presidential disaster declaration was signed for the repeated rounds of severe weather that hit northeast Iowa in the middle of June. This disaster declaration included Allamakee, Chickasaw and Fayette Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains flooded basements in Ossian.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary extended northeast to southwest across Iowa.  Moisture pooled along the boundary with precipitable water values rising to over 2 inches during the night.  An MCS developed over Nebraska into western Iowa.  In what was a classic Maddox |Flash Flood event, heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches occurred in just over an hour over Crawford County.  Flowing water closed U.S. Highway 30 and some of the nearby roads.  Extensive low land flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing over the road near Team Ford on U.S. Highway 30, at least 6 inches deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches or more of water was moving rapidly in areas that are not typical.  Two and one half inches of rain fell in 45 minutes.  The Little Beaver Creek was out of its banks.

There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.

A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.

The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over 340th Avenue at the confluence of two small creeks north of Ruthven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided very early in the month on the Turkey River but continued until the middle of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late June on the Mississippi River in Guttenberg continued into the second week of July before the river went below the flood stage. The river crested 2 feet above the flood stage at 17 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a multiple segment tornado with two segment including portions of Marshall and Hardin counties.  This is the second and final segment in Hardin county.  After crossing into Hardin county, the narrowing tornado left a highly convergent tree damage pattern across 335th St.  The path washed out in a pasture NE of this point just west of the Iowa River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4.07 inches in past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed north of Martensdale and quickly tracked to the SE, reaching its widest and strongest point just east of the city where it damaged dozens of trees. The surface circulation weakened as it ascended a 100 foot high ridge south of the Middle River and then dissipated about a half mile south of the ridge crest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and severe winds combined to produce damage to crop and houses.  A semi was blown over on Interstate 80 near mile marker 71 by the strong winds.  The wind driven hail stripped crops across the far northeast corner of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary extended northeast to southwest across Iowa.  Moisture pooled along the boundary with precipitable water values rising to over 2 inches during the night.  An MCS developed over Nebraska into western Iowa.  In what was a classic Maddox |Flash Flood event, heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches occurred in just over an hour over Crawford County.  Flowing water closed U.S. Highway 30 and some of the nearby roads.  Extensive low land flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing over the road at 7th street and Highway 30 at least six inched deep.  Road barricaded.

Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boone County EM reports golf ball size hail with tree branches coming down in strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable wind along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout photographed by several members of the public.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Narrow but strong RFD west and south of the beginning of a tornado track. Damage path started just west of the tornado track and then shifted south of the track. Over a dozen trees were uprooted and many more had limb damage. A few very small and old farm outbuildings were destroyed. Path of damage was just under three miles long and 1/2 of a mile wide, though the worst damage corridor was 100-150 yards wide. Point for Storm Data is in the area of strongest damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad area of inflow winds north of the tornado. Damage was mostly to large tree limbs. Damage first noted near county road G4R and Settlers Ave and grew narrower and more intense as it approached the tornado. Area of strongest damage denoted by Storm Data point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very narrow but intense RFD south of the tornado path. Damage path only about a mile long and 100-150 yards wide before it wraps into the tornado. Divergent wind pattern throughout with dozens of trees snapped and uprooted. One house sustained roof and siding damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swath of winds downstream of a dissipating tornado. Worst damage was at the beginning, where an outbuilding was very heavily damaged and debris thrown east across a road. The damage intensity lessened and the path broadened within a few miles SE of the start point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave moved through the central U.S. during the day on the 28th.  A squall line developed over central Iowa and moved east during the afternoon.  The storms produced small hail, very heavy rainfall, and gusty winds.  One storm produced winds in Tama County at Traer.  Several trees were toppled and damage was done to the roof of one home on the south side of town.  There were also other scattered reports of damage in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of several trees down in Traer with roof damage to a home on the south side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pole shed was destroyed northeast of Boyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary extended northeast to southwest across Iowa.  Moisture pooled along the boundary with precipitable water values rising to over 2 inches during the night.  An MCS developed over Nebraska into western Iowa.  In what was a classic Maddox |Flash Flood event, heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches occurred in just over an hour over Crawford County.  Flowing water closed U.S. Highway 30 and some of the nearby roads.  Extensive low land flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water at least a foot deep flowing through yards in the southwest part of a trailer park east of Denison in the Lincoln Highway drainage area. Water overflowing park entrance area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total of 2.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong theta-e advection took place ahead of a warm front southwest of Iowa.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the front and produced a band of heavy rainfall from north central and parts of northwest Iowa into the southeast counties.  Rainfall of two to nearly five inches was reported.  A rainfall report of 4.68 inches was received east of Ringsted in Emmet County.  No severe weather was reported, however nickel size hail fell north of Ankeny in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea size hail nearly covered the ground with a few stones as large as nickels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4.68 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public picture of tornado west of Tripoli that remained in open agricultural fields for the duration of its path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported around 190 homes in Adair were damaged by large hail driven by strong to severe wind.  Numerous trees were damaged.  Crops shredded by the wind driven hail.  Cars on I-80 had broken windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down with a debris ball noted.  Damage to homes occurred many to the southwest of Reinbeck.  Numerous crops flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over the intersection of 280th Street and 460th Avenue due to the Jack Creek overflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois the evening of August 4, as a weak cold front slowly pushed south over the region. One storm surpassed severe levels, producing quarter sized hail in Coralville, IA just before 7 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Twitter user reported that quarter sized hail fell in Coralville, IA at 653 pm CDT Aug 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois August 25, sparking some scattered showers and thunderstorms over areas north of Interstate 80.  Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds from Clinton County in Iowa to Whiteside and Henry Counties in Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 60 mph blew down some tree branches in Clinton, IA.  This report was posted on Facebook and relayed by the Clinton County EMA.

A disturbance moving across northern Iowa on July 22 lead to the development of thunderstorms shortly after sunrise in eastern Iowa. One of these thunderstorms became severe and produced mainly dime to nickel sized hail with a few stones as large as quarters as it moved across Washington County.  There thunderstorms also produced heavy rain across eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Fire Department reported half inch diameter hail with a few that were quarter sized. Time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.

Strong theta-e advection took place ahead of a warm front southwest of Iowa.  Thunderstorms formed ahead of the front and produced a band of heavy rainfall from north central and parts of northwest Iowa into the southeast counties.  Rainfall of two to nearly five inches was reported.  A rainfall report of 4.68 inches was received east of Ringsted in Emmet County.  No severe weather was reported, however nickel size hail fell north of Ankeny in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3.6 inches of rainfall.

The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to tennis ball size hail just south of town.

A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visually striking tornado moved through rural areas of Tama county. Debris was noted, farmstead was damaged, and numerous crops damaged.  Track was well captured by satellite overflight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought a round of light to briefly moderate snow to much of the area on November 15, 2014.  This was the first widespread accumulating snow of the season with the snow lasting only about 3 to 6 hours at most locations. Winds were light which prevented any blowing or drifting. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches north of Interstate 80 in northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois.  To the south across southeast Iowa, extreme western Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri 1 to 2 inches was reported.  The highest snow amount of 4 inches was measured near Anamosa, Iowa in Jones county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.5 inches of snow at Wellman.

A cold front moved southeast into Iowa during the evening of the 19th.  A low level jet of 40 to 45 kts transported moisture into Iowa ahead of the front with surface dewpoints reaching the mid 60s and precipitable water values increasing to 1.7 inches ahead of the front.  The freezing level was quite high for mid September at around 14,500 feet.  Many of the storms produced hail, but size was limited in spite of the available CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere being around 500 J/kg.  The atmosphere was quite unstable with 2000 J/kg of CAPE present.  The downdraft CAPE was in the 500 to 800 J/kg range.  The atmosphere was fairly strongly sheared with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear available.  The LCL was around 1000 meters.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the front over northwest Iowa.  Much of the severe weather associated with them occurred to the northwest of the Des Moines CWA.  One area of the line became stronger and exhibited a beta bow element.  This produced high winds in the Ruthven area of Palo Alto County.  The winds toppled several 24 inch diameter trees and toppled several power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palo Alto County EM reported several 24 inch diameter trees downed along with several power lines down in Ruthven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south across Iowa during the night of the 14th into the morning of the 15th.  There was marginal instability...however a low level jet set up overnight and resulted in the development of thunderstorms across southern Iowa  Though most were not severe, one storm produced quarter size hail as it moved across Wayne and Appanoose Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed over much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois around midnight the morning of the 14th and lingered into the day.  Atmospheric mixing driven by the sun caused the fog to lift during the day of the 14th in areas generally south of U.S. Highway 30.  Areas along the U.S. Highway 20 corridor, however, remained under a blanket of dense fog through the day and into the next morning.  Fog finally lifted around mid morning on the 15th.  At least one accident with injuries was reported in the Dubuque area, which may have been indirectly related to the fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog persisted through the day.  According to a local TV station report, eight people, including 6 children, were injured in a car accident on Highway 20 near Peosta just before 2 pm.
