EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of rainfall.

The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy damage to a machine shed and trees caused by strong straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought a round of light to briefly moderate snow to much of the area on November 15, 2014.  This was the first widespread accumulating snow of the season with the snow lasting only about 3 to 6 hours at most locations. Winds were light which prevented any blowing or drifting. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches north of Interstate 80 in northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois.  To the south across southeast Iowa, extreme western Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri 1 to 2 inches was reported.  The highest snow amount of 4 inches was measured near Anamosa, Iowa in Jones county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 4.0 inches of snow 6 miles southwest of Anamosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.26 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought a round of light to briefly moderate snow to much of the area on November 15, 2014.  This was the first widespread accumulating snow of the season with the snow lasting only about 3 to 6 hours at most locations. Winds were light which prevented any blowing or drifting. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches north of Interstate 80 in northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois.  To the south across southeast Iowa, extreme western Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri 1 to 2 inches was reported.  The highest snow amount of 4 inches was measured near Anamosa, Iowa in Jones county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Donnellson measured 2.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total for the past 24 hours was 2.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The season's first strong synoptic weather system moved through the central U.S. on the 13th into the 14th.  The storm developed over the southern U.S. and moved from Oklahoma on the 13th into Illinois on the morning of the 14th.  The system brought heavy rainfall to the east half of Iowa.  Rainfall caused stream rises, but no major flooding occurred.  Though moisture was drawn into the state, Iowa was clearly in the deformation zone of the storms  The low level jet increased to around 40 kts, resulting in precipitable water values of nearly two standard deviation units above normal in the 1 to 1.25 inch range.  Widespread rainfall amounts of one to two inches occurred over the east half of Iowa with a narrow band of 2 to 4 inch rainfall from south central into parts of east central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall was southeast of Pella in Marion County with 4.28 inches recorded.  The heavy rain caused minor flooding along the mainstem Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa.  The river crested one to one and one half feet over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain duration was 24 hours with 3.20 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.23 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The season's first strong synoptic weather system moved through the central U.S. on the 13th into the 14th.  The storm developed over the southern U.S. and moved from Oklahoma on the 13th into Illinois on the morning of the 14th.  The system brought heavy rainfall to the east half of Iowa.  Rainfall caused stream rises, but no major flooding occurred.  Though moisture was drawn into the state, Iowa was clearly in the deformation zone of the storms  The low level jet increased to around 40 kts, resulting in precipitable water values of nearly two standard deviation units above normal in the 1 to 1.25 inch range.  Widespread rainfall amounts of one to two inches occurred over the east half of Iowa with a narrow band of 2 to 4 inch rainfall from south central into parts of east central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall was southeast of Pella in Marion County with 4.28 inches recorded.  The heavy rain caused minor flooding along the mainstem Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa.  The river crested one to one and one half feet over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain duration was 24 hours with 4.28 inches reported.

A large area of rain and thunderstorms developed ahead of this trough on the north/cool side of an approaching warm frontal boundary located to the southwest of the state.  Heavy rainfall occurred with several locations receiving 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rainfall.  The heaviest report was a 3.40 inch amount east of Manning in Carroll County.   The heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding along the Raccoon River with the river cresting about six tenths of a foot above flood stage at Fleur Drive in Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from overnight was 1.62 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The season's first strong synoptic weather system moved through the central U.S. on the 13th into the 14th.  The storm developed over the southern U.S. and moved from Oklahoma on the 13th into Illinois on the morning of the 14th.  The system brought heavy rainfall to the east half of Iowa.  Rainfall caused stream rises, but no major flooding occurred.  Though moisture was drawn into the state, Iowa was clearly in the deformation zone of the storms  The low level jet increased to around 40 kts, resulting in precipitable water values of nearly two standard deviation units above normal in the 1 to 1.25 inch range.  Widespread rainfall amounts of one to two inches occurred over the east half of Iowa with a narrow band of 2 to 4 inch rainfall from south central into parts of east central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall was southeast of Pella in Marion County with 4.28 inches recorded.  The heavy rain caused minor flooding along the mainstem Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa.  The river crested one to one and one half feet over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain duration was 24 hours with at total of 3.35 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall for the past 24 hours was 4.20 inches.

A large area of rain and thunderstorms developed ahead of this trough on the north/cool side of an approaching warm frontal boundary located to the southwest of the state.  Heavy rainfall occurred with several locations receiving 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rainfall.  The heaviest report was a 3.40 inch amount east of Manning in Carroll County.   The heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding along the Raccoon River with the river cresting about six tenths of a foot above flood stage at Fleur Drive in Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.28 inches in the past 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall since 1500 CST on the 30th was 1.98 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system dug southeastward through the state with persistent light to moderate snowfall occurring during the early morning hours on the 26th and continuing through the day before tapering off around late afternoon/early evening on the 26th. Winds were generally light with little impacts due to the longer duration light to moderate snowfall. An initial band of moderate snow moved through during the overnight hours in the warm air advection region of the system as warmer, moist air wrapped up around the low pressure area. As the system pivoted to the east/southeast through the day, light snowfall persisted with another moderate to heavier band of snow falling in the mid afternoon hours as the colder air pushed in on the backside of the system. Northwest winds picked up after the snow ended with some minor drifting of snow reported on roadways. Total snowfall amounts for the area were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range across central Iowa with a few higher amounts across extreme north central Iowa where 9 inches was reported at Ringsted in Emmet County. At Emmetsburg in Palo Alto County, 7.3 inches was reported, and 7.0 inches fell at both Titonka and Algona in Kossuth County and at Goldfield in Wright County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 9 inches was reported by the observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water was over the road on Dubuque Dr about one and one half mile west of Highway 163 near Otley.

There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bull Creek was out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough of low pressure moved out of the western U.S. into the central states on the morning of the 1st of October.  A large area of rain and thunderstorms developed ahead of this trough on the north/cool side of an approaching warm frontal boundary located to the southwest of the state.  Heavy rainfall occurred with several locations receiving 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rainfall.  The heaviest report was a 3.40 inch amount east of Manning in Carroll County.   The heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding along the Raccoon River with the river cresting about six tenths of a foot above flood stage at Fleur Drive in Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.18 inches in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.85 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally wet period with repeated thunderstorms from August into early September led to a number of area rivers having above normal river levels. A slow moving complex of thunderstorms near the 10th of September set rivers in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa above flood stage. This included the Missouri and the Big Blue Rivers in Nebraska, and the East Nishnabotna, main stem of the Nishnabotna, and Nodaway Rivers in southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm complex lead to heavy rainfall on the 9th causing the East Nishnabotna River to rise above flood stage around the Riverton area. The river rose above flood stage during the afternoon on the 10th, crested during the morning on the 11th, and fell below flood stage later that afternoon. The flooding was minor and confined to low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation occurred across most of the region during the month of August. A particularly wet period occurred at the end of the month though as two days of repeated heavy rainfall led to river flooding across parts of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Most of the flooding was minor, but did cause some road closures and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated thunderstorms on the 27th and 28th of August caused heavy rainfall and the Nodaway River to go into flood. The river rose above flood stage on the evening of the 28th, the river created during the afternoon of the 29th, and fell below flood stage in the early morning of the 30th. The flooding was generally minor and confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Muchakinock Creek remained out of its banks 1.5 miles west of Highway 163 on Highway 92. The road was closed.

A strong upper level trough of low pressure moved out of the western U.S. into the central states on the morning of the 1st of October.  A large area of rain and thunderstorms developed ahead of this trough on the north/cool side of an approaching warm frontal boundary located to the southwest of the state.  Heavy rainfall occurred with several locations receiving 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rainfall.  The heaviest report was a 3.40 inch amount east of Manning in Carroll County.   The heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding along the Raccoon River with the river cresting about six tenths of a foot above flood stage at Fleur Drive in Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.58 inches in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.26 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated thunderstorms in late August led to flooding along several rivers in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Some of the rivers remained in flood into early September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of August allowed the West Nishnabotna River to rise above flood stage on the early morning on the 2st, crest during the late morning, and fall below flood stage during the afternoon. The flooding was minor and confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed over Kansas and moved into southern Iowa during the 15th into the early morning hours of the 16th.  Warm air advection took place and warmer, moist air lifted north over a polar airmass that was in place over Iowa.  Snow developed across the state.  Though the snow did not fall heavily, it was persistent.  A snowfall total of 6 inches was reported at Graettinger in Palo Alto County and around 5 inches of snow was reported in areas of Emmet and Kossuth Counties.  The impact was reduced as the snow was dry in nature and the winds were light through the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from social media.

The season's first strong synoptic weather system moved through the central U.S. on the 13th into the 14th.  The storm developed over the southern U.S. and moved from Oklahoma on the 13th into Illinois on the morning of the 14th.  The system brought heavy rainfall to the east half of Iowa.  Rainfall caused stream rises, but no major flooding occurred.  Though moisture was drawn into the state, Iowa was clearly in the deformation zone of the storms  The low level jet increased to around 40 kts, resulting in precipitable water values of nearly two standard deviation units above normal in the 1 to 1.25 inch range.  Widespread rainfall amounts of one to two inches occurred over the east half of Iowa with a narrow band of 2 to 4 inch rainfall from south central into parts of east central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall was southeast of Pella in Marion County with 4.28 inches recorded.  The heavy rain caused minor flooding along the mainstem Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa.  The river crested one to one and one half feet over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall today through 800 PM CST was 1.23 inches.

There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.62 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were the high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This caused by a dam failure, which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and lowland flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties. The heavy rain also led to river flooding along the Raccoon River at Fleur Drive in Des Moines. The river was in flood for around 1 day and crested at 2 feet above flood stage. Additional heavy rainfall reports can be found in the 31 August 2014 event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Midwest Feb. 20 bringing showers and scattered thunderstorms to Eastern Iowa, Northwest Illinois, and Northeast Missouri.  Some of the storms were severe producing damaging winds and large hail.  Seasonally moderate to heavy rainfall totals were observed, with amounts generally between 1 and 3 inches.  The combination of frozen ground, heavy rains, and snow melt resulted in flooding in several areas.  Behind the system, strong gradient winds of 25 to 40 mph were observed, with some gusts over 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some power lines were blown down on Benton Street around 2 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms that developed along the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in eastern Nebraska moved into western Iowa during the evening of May 11th.  These storms spawned tornadoes in Nebraska, but in western Iowa they mainly dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail that reached around baseball-size was reported from southwest through west of the Thurman area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure moved across northern Missouri during the afternoon.  There was some instability as strong theta-e advection occurred during the afternoon.  CAPE was limited with only about 500 J/kg available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 200 to 300 J/kg range.  With the freezing level well below 10,000 feet, thunderstorms that did develop produced hail quickly.  One storm became severe as it moved across Marshall County and dropped nickel to quarter size hail.

A strong low pressure moved across northern Missouri during the afternoon.  There was some instability as strong theta-e advection occurred during the afternoon.  CAPE was limited with only about 500 J/kg available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 200 to 300 J/kg range.  With the freezing level well below 10,000 feet, thunderstorms that did develop produced hail quickly.  One storm became severe as it moved across Marshall County and dropped nickel to quarter size hail.

Low pressure that was located over northwest Kansas the previous day moved to eastern Nebraska on the 28th. A warm front extended east-southeast from the low into Illinois.  A marked dry line was present by mid-day on the 28th, approaching Iowa from the southwest.  Thunderstorms formed in an arc along the dry line by early afternoon.  The atmosphere was not as conducive to severe weather as it was the previous day.  There was around 1000 J/kg of CAPE available, with 500 J/kg of downdraft CAPE. The freezing level was only 9,500 feet, however available CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer was only 200 to 400 J/kg.  Precipitable water values were down to 0.8 inches with the drier air moving into the area.  The shear was weak with 20 to 30 kts of effective shear.  It was fortunate that other severe parameters were weak as the Lifted Index was between -9 and -12 C and the LCL only 500 meters by early in the afternoon.  The line of storms brought gusty winds, hail, and brief heavy rainfall.  The hail reports received were below severe levels with a few reports of penny to nickel size hail received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel hail reported with torrential rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms moved across western Iowa on Sunday morning April 27th, and again developed along a nearly stationary boundary on Monday afternoon April 28th. Some of these storms produced small hail.

A slow moving cold front pushing southeast across the area produced a band of snow behind the front on March 1-2, 2014.  Light to moderate snow was observed with amounts of 3 to 6 inches common across eastern Iowa, northwest and western Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri. The highest amount reported was 6 inches near Calamus in eastern Iowa and at Memphis, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 4.2 inches of snow 1 mile southeast of Vinton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported hail reached at least quarter-size.  The hail was accompanied by wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over southwest Kansas during the night of the 19th into the day on the 20th.  The low turned northeast through Missouri into east central Iowa by the afternoon of the 20th.  A strong surge of moisture pushed north ahead of the low with dewpoint temperatures in the 40s to low 50s to the south of the low pressure track.  Northwest of the low, temperatures were quite mild for late February, generally in the low to mid 30s.  The storm began as very wet snow.  The wet snow continued into the evening hours and caused power outages.  At one point during the night, Alliant energy had over 16,000 customers across northern IA and southern MN without power. Many of the small towns across the north and portions of Mason City were without power from the storm (either tree limbs down on power lines or power lines down). Also as many as 650 were out across central and southern Iowa. MidAmerican Energy reported over 600 out in Fort Dodge and over 300 out in Waterloo overnight. There were still 6000 out across the north with Alliant Energy and 14 out with MidAmerican in Waterloo as the sun rose on the 21st.  Colder air began to move in during the evening hours.  The sub-freezing temperatures changed the texture of the snow. Winds increased gradually during the afternoon and evening hours.  Sustained winds were 25 to 35 MPH with gusts of 40 to 50 MPH through much of the night.  Conditions deteriorated rapidly through the night.  Interstate 35 was closed north of Ames and north into southern Minnesota due to blizzard conditions. A portion of Highway 69 became impassable in Hamilton county due to drifting snow.  The Iowa DOT pulled snow plows off of the roads during the blizzard with visibility reduced to zero.  Many areas received 3 to 6 inches of snow from the event.  Heaviest snowfall was over north central Iowa with some of the heavier amounts including 10.5 inches in Hancock at Britt, 9.5 inches in Wright County at Belmond, and 7.5 inches in Worth County at Manly and in Cerro Gordo County at Meservey.

Low pressure developed over southwest Kansas during the night of the 19th into the day on the 20th.  The low turned northeast through Missouri into east central Iowa by the afternoon of the 20th.  A strong surge of moisture pushed north ahead of the low with dewpoint temperatures in the 40s to low 50s to the south of the low pressure track.  Northwest of the low, temperatures were quite mild for late February, generally in the low to mid 30s.  The storm began as very wet snow.  The wet snow continued into the evening hours and caused power outages.  At one point during the night, Alliant energy had over 16,000 customers across northern IA and southern MN without power. Many of the small towns across the north and portions of Mason City were without power from the storm (either tree limbs down on power lines or power lines down). Also as many as 650 were out across central and southern Iowa. MidAmerican Energy reported over 600 out in Fort Dodge and over 300 out in Waterloo overnight. There were still 6000 out across the north with Alliant Energy and 14 out with MidAmerican in Waterloo as the sun rose on the 21st.  Colder air began to move in during the evening hours.  The sub-freezing temperatures changed the texture of the snow. Winds increased gradually during the afternoon and evening hours.  Sustained winds were 25 to 35 MPH with gusts of 40 to 50 MPH through much of the night.  Conditions deteriorated rapidly through the night.  Interstate 35 was closed north of Ames and north into southern Minnesota due to blizzard conditions. A portion of Highway 69 became impassable in Hamilton county due to drifting snow.  The Iowa DOT pulled snow plows off of the roads during the blizzard with visibility reduced to zero.  Many areas received 3 to 6 inches of snow from the event.  Heaviest snowfall was over north central Iowa with some of the heavier amounts including 10.5 inches in Hancock at Britt, 9.5 inches in Wright County at Belmond, and 7.5 inches in Worth County at Manly and in Cerro Gordo County at Meservey.

A long lasting winter storm affected southern Iowa on the 4th and 5th of February.  The surface low was well to the south and east of Iowa.  A large deformation area formed well to the north and northwest of the low, deep in the cold air over northern Missouri and southern Iowa.  The overall dynamics were weak, leading to a long-lasting period of light to moderate snow.  Snowfall totals were generally in the 5 to 8 inch range over a period of 24 to 30 hours.  The heaviest snowfall was over the far southern part of Iowa.  Some of the heavier totals include 8 inches in Decatur County at Lamoni, and in Appanoose County at Centerville.  Seven inches fell in Warren County at Indianola, and in Mahaska County at Oskaloosa.  Although snowfall amounts were marginal to below criteria in and of itself, the impacts were more significant due to the cold temperatures and strong north wind.  Temperatures fell into the single digits across the area with sustained north winds of 15 to 20 MPH, with gusts of 30 to 35 MPH for several hours.  Heavy drifting of snow took place with areas of significant visibility restrictions due to blowing snow.  Many roads were severely drifted with some road closures reported.  Most of the schools in the area were either cancelled or on delayed start.  Some businesses were closed for the day and some activities cancelled as well.

A slow moving cold front pushing southeast across the area produced a band of snow behind the front on March 1-2, 2014.  Light to moderate snow was observed with amounts of 3 to 6 inches common across eastern Iowa, northwest and western Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri. The highest amount reported was 6 inches near Calamus in eastern Iowa and at Memphis, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.9 inches of snow at Donnellson.

A strong upper level disturbance produced bands of moderate to locally heavy snowfall on March 4-5, 2014.  The heaviest bands of snow set up along Interstate 80 from about the Quad Cities to Princeton, Illinois and along Highway 20 from Dubuque, Iowa to Freeport, Illinois.  In these two bands, snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were common.  The highest amount reported was 3.7 inches at the Quad Cities International Airport at Moline, Illinois and at Coal Valley, Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Dubuque Regional Airport measured 3.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed over northeast Iowa during the evening of March 4th and continued into the morning of the 5th. This band of snow remained nearly stationary as an upper level storm system moved over the area. Snow amounts of 6 to 8 inches were reported across all or portions of Mitchell, Howard, Winneshiek and Allamakee Counties. The highest reported total was 8.8 inches northwest of Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 9 inches of snow across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 8.8 inches northwest of Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms that developed along the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in eastern Nebraska moved into western Iowa during the evening of May 11th.  These storms spawned tornadoes in Nebraska, but in western Iowa they mainly dropped large hail.

Supercell thunderstorms that developed along the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in eastern Nebraska moved into western Iowa during the evening of May 11th.  These storms spawned tornadoes in Nebraska, but in western Iowa they mainly dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball-size hail was reported by emergency management a little south of Red Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Midwest Feb. 20 bringing showers and scattered thunderstorms to Eastern Iowa, Northwest Illinois, and Northeast Missouri.  Some of the storms were severe producing damaging winds and large hail.  Seasonally moderate to heavy rainfall totals were observed, with amounts generally between 1 and 3 inches.  The combination of frozen ground, heavy rains, and snow melt resulted in flooding in several areas.  Behind the system, strong gradient winds of 25 to 40 mph were observed, with some gusts over 50 mph.

An unseasonably strong mid and upper level jet combined with high amounts of low level moisture and instability to create a volatile atmosphere across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa late in the night and into Monday morning. Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low-level jet and quickly became severe. Isolated to scattered supercells moved across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa through late morning before exiting into central Iowa.

A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the mid Mississippi valley created an environment favorable for funnel clouds.  A few funnel clouds developed in association with showers in the cold air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two funnel clouds developed in the hills area within 5 to 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported shingles blown off of houses, siding blown off of a church, and small limbs of 2 to 3 inches in diameter were down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager for Johnson County reported a wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph on Interstate 80 west of Coraville near Tiffin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of June 14th produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding. The thunderstorms also produced damaging winds and hail. The heavy rain also caused longer term lowland flooding of fields and other low areas including roads, that lasted well into the daytime hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of over 2 inches in a half hour caused flash flooding of streets in Sioux City. The flooding blew off manhole covers.

A very warm airmass moved into Iowa on the 11th and 12th of April.  By the afternoon of the 12th, temperatures reached the 80s across a large part of Iowa with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 50s to around 60.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon and tracked southeast across the state during the evening and overnight hours.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE values increasing to 2000 to 2500 J/kg by late afternoon.   Downdraft CAPE rose to between 800 and 1200 J/kg, while CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 400 to 500 J/kg range  The freezing level was around 12,000 ft near the storms inception point.  Precipitable water values increased to just over an inch by evening.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 40 to 60 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms were initially capped off during much of the afternoon with only scattered storms forming along the front. The cap was broken by late afternoon into the evening. A line of strong to severe storms formed.  The primary mode of severe weather was hail with most storms producing at least some hail.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  A few of the storms produced larger hail, with 2.5 inch diameter hail reported in Story County, and 2 inch diameter hail in Butler and Bremer Counties.  After sunset, the storms began to produce severe winds with several gusts to around 60 MPH reported over the southeast third of the state.  As the winds increased, the hail became smaller.  Significant damage was done in Mahaska County by the strong winds.  Heavy straight-line winds caused several thousand dollars in damage to a farm.  The barn roof was damaged, a south wall partially gone, overhead door destroyed and wrapped around a trailer, skylights blown out, north wall section bowed out, and a 12' X 14' door was blown off. Three fruit trees were also blown down.  Roof damage was reported in Wayne County as very strong straight-line winds swept through the Promise City area.  In addition, a large shed was blown down by the winds south of Corydon.  During the night into the next morning the storms became multi-cellular and began to train over the state as the next shortwave advanced northeast toward the central U.S.  Locally heavy rain was reported with a 3.5 inch total in a 6-hour period reported in Clarke County of south central Iowa.  Twenty-four hour rainfall totals in Jasper and Poweshiek Counties were around 4 inches.  Two-day totals across much of the southeast half of Iowa were in the two to five inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall for the two days was 3.37 inches.

By the afternoon of the 12th, temperatures reached the 80s across a large part of Iowa with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 50s to around 60.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon and tracked southeast across the state during the evening and overnight hours.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE values increasing to 2000 to 2500 J/kg by late afternoon.   Downdraft CAPE rose to between 800 and 1200 J/kg, while CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 400 to 500 J/kg range  The freezing level was around 12,000 ft near the storms inception point.  Precipitable water values increased to just over an inch by evening.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 40 to 60 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms were initially capped off during much of the afternoon with only scattered storms forming along the front. The cap was broken by late afternoon into the evening. A line of strong to severe storms formed.  The primary mode of severe weather was hail with most storms producing at least some hail.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  A few of the storms produced larger hail, with 2.5 inch diameter hail reported in Story County, and 2 inch diameter hail in Butler and Bremer Counties.  After sunset, the storms began to produce severe winds with several gusts to around 60 MPH reported over the southeast third of the state.  As the winds increased, the hail became smaller.  Significant damage was done in Mahaska County by the strong winds.  Heavy straight-line winds caused several thousand dollars in damage to a farm.  The barn roof was damaged, a south wall partially gone, overhead door destroyed and wrapped around a trailer, skylights blown out, north wall section bowed out, and a 12' X 14' door was blown off. Three fruit trees were also blown down.  Roof damage was reported in Wayne County as very strong straight-line winds swept through the Promise City area.  In addition, a large shed was blown down by the winds south of Corydon.  During the night into the next morning the storms became multi-cellular and began to train over the state as the next shortwave advanced northeast toward the central U.S.  Locally heavy rain was reported with a 3.5 inch total in a 6-hour period reported in Clarke County of south central Iowa.  Twenty-four hour rainfall totals in Jasper and Poweshiek Counties were around 4 inches.  Two-day totals across much of the southeast half of Iowa were in the two to five inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in the late afternoon and early evening of May 11th produced large hail and damaging winds in northwest Iowa, as well as two tornadoes which caused damage. The severe weather occurred in Cherokee, O'Brien, and Sioux Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damaged 3 farmsteads in including damage to at least one house. The greatest damage was to structures and vehicles at a dairy farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual severe thunderstorm environment developed across the region April 2nd into the 3rd with surface temperatures during the event in the 35 to 45 degree range. A disturbance moving across the region produced scattered showers and thunderstorms overnight with an isolated thunderstorms becoming severe over eastern Lousia  and Benton Counties and produced mainly golf ball sized hail with baseball sized hail reported in the town of Wapello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager in Burlington reported dime to penny size hail falling 300 yards east of the Burlington Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media relayed a public report of golf ball to baseball sized hail covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located to the south of Iowa during the day on the 26th.  A steady flow of moisture impinged on the front through the day.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.5 to 1.7 inches during the day.  Very little shear was present, under 20 kts.  CAPE was around 2000 J/kg with 400 J/kg of CAPE on the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 500 to 1000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms formed in the morning over central Iowa.  They continued to redevelop and train over the area through much of the day.  The event was mainly a heavy rain event with many areas of Polk and Dallas Counties receiving 2 to 6.5 inches of rain during the day.  Flash flooding occurred in Dallas County.  There were a few storms that produced hail.  One inch diameter hail fell in Dallas County, and nickel size hail fell in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.95 inches so far.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Benton County Emergency Manager reported a building blown down along with power lines. a tractor trailer was jack-knifed on Highway 30.

A storm system lifted into northern Iowa during the day on April 12, 2014 and dragged cold front across eastern Iowa. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of nickel sized hail reported in Fairbank by the son of a COOP Observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of June 14th produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding. The thunderstorms also produced damaging winds and hail. The heavy rain also caused longer term lowland flooding of fields and other low areas including roads, that lasted well into the daytime hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of several roads and low areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced numerous events of large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and areal flooding across most of northwest Iowa from late morning through the evening of June 16th. There was also one brief tornado. The areal flooding was a longer term event and persisted through much of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on April 12th. These storms developed along a very slow moving cold front and primarily produced large hail, although some occasional high winds also were reported. The first round of storms moved in during the mid morning hours with the last of the storms ending during the early evening. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized near Westgate (Fayette County), Elgin (Fayette County) and Rockford (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to walnut sized hail fell in the city of Fayette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southeast U.S. during the day on the 8th.  Surface low pressure was located over northwest Kansas in the morning and tracked northeast into southern Minnesota by the evening.  Moisture was drawn north ahead of the low with precipitable water values rising to around 1.3 inches.  Surface dewpoints rose into the low to mid 60s.  The atmosphere was unstable with the lifted index falling to -6 C and CAPE values around 2000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1200 J/kg range.  The flow was around 40 kts at 850 mb from the south-southwest.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was relatively high around 1250 meters.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was around 400 J/kg with the freezing level only 12,000 feet.  Thunderstorms developed over west central into northwest Iowa along a cold front that pushed into the state.  The storms produced winds of up to 70 MPH in Kossuth County near Algona.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in Carroll County west of Willey.  A severe thunderstorm developed over south central Iowa southwest of the Des Moines metro area.  This storm produced large hail with a swath of hail extending form Madison County through the Des Moines area before it weakened.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  Baseball size hail fell on the northwest side of the Metro east of Urbandale.  The storm also produced gusty winds in the 55 to 60 mph range reported at a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 57 MPH was also measured.

The barn roof was damaged, a south wall partially gone, overhead door destroyed and wrapped around a trailer, skylights blown out, north wall section bowed out, and a 12' X 14' door was blown off. Three fruit trees were also blown down.  Roof damage was reported in Wayne County as very strong straight-line winds swept through the Promise City area.  In addition, a large shed was blown down by the winds south of Corydon.  During the night into the next morning the storms became multi-cellular and began to train over the state as the next shortwave advanced northeast toward the central U.S.  Locally heavy rain was reported with a 3.5 inch total in a 6-hour period reported in Clarke County of south central Iowa.  Twenty-four hour rainfall totals in Jasper and Poweshiek Counties were around 4 inches.  Two-day totals across much of the southeast half of Iowa were in the two to five inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey teams determined that a tornado developed in far northern Wapello County, crossed Keokuk County and southeastern Iowa County, and ended in rural southwest Johnson County.  This entry is for the portion of the tornado that affected Keokuk County.  ||This long track tornado started east of Highland Center and traveled at about 60 mph to the northeast. The circulation was initially very broad and began to tighten as it progressed to the northeast.  The tornado moved into Keokuk County at around 323 pm CDT (223 pm CST).  It then created a near-continuous damage path across Keokuk County, with damage primarily to  outbuildings, trees, and power poles.  In Martinsburg, a tied-down mobile home was also pushed to the north off its block base.   Two fatalities occurred, one west of Martinsburg and on north of Kinross.  Both fatalities were associated with people who were outdoors and caught in debris from outbuildings that were destroyed.  ||The tornado was on the ground for about 30 miles across Keokuk County.  It exited Keokuk County at about 352 pm and moved into Iowa County near the junction of the Keokuk-Iowa-Washington county lines.

After crossing Keokuk County, the tornado moved into Iowa County near the junction of the Iowa, Keokuk, and Washington County lines at around 352 pm CDT (252 pm CST). It left a damage path about 10 miles long in rural areas that included damage to outbuildings and trees. ||The tornado exited Iowa County at about 402 pm CDT (302 pm CST) and moved into Johnson County a few miles south of I-80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located to the south of Iowa during the day on the 26th.  A steady flow of moisture impinged on the front through the day.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.5 to 1.7 inches during the day.  Very little shear was present, under 20 kts.  CAPE was around 2000 J/kg with 400 J/kg of CAPE on the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 500 to 1000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms formed in the morning over central Iowa.  They continued to redevelop and train over the area through much of the day.  The event was mainly a heavy rain event with many areas of Polk and Dallas Counties receiving 2 to 6.5 inches of rain during the day.  Flash flooding occurred in Dallas County.  There were a few storms that produced hail.  One inch diameter hail fell in Dallas County, and nickel size hail fell in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty four hour rainfall total of 4.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted into northern Iowa during the day on April 12, 2014 and dragged cold front across eastern Iowa. This lead to the development of a line of thunderstorms that moved across eastern Iowa with hail up to the size of nickels and damaging winds mainly along and to the north of Interstate 80. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported that power poles were snapped and a hog building lost its roof near North English. This report was delayed and the time was estimated to have occurred between 11 and 1130 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on April 12th. These storms developed along a very slow moving cold front and primarily produced large hail, although some occasional high winds also were reported. The first round of storms moved in during the mid morning hours with the last of the storms ending during the early evening. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized near Westgate (Fayette County), Elgin (Fayette County) and Rockford (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in Rockford.

A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southeast U.S. during the day on the 8th.  Surface low pressure was located over northwest Kansas in the morning and tracked northeast into southern Minnesota by the evening.  Moisture was drawn north ahead of the low with precipitable water values rising to around 1.3 inches.  Surface dewpoints rose into the low to mid 60s.  The atmosphere was unstable with the lifted index falling to -6 C and CAPE values around 2000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1200 J/kg range.  The flow was around 40 kts at 850 mb from the south-southwest.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was relatively high around 1250 meters.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was around 400 J/kg with the freezing level only 12,000 feet.  Thunderstorms developed over west central into northwest Iowa along a cold front that pushed into the state.  The storms produced winds of up to 70 MPH in Kossuth County near Algona.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in Carroll County west of Willey.  A severe thunderstorm developed over south central Iowa southwest of the Des Moines metro area.  This storm produced large hail with a swath of hail extending form Madison County through the Des Moines area before it weakened.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  Baseball size hail fell on the northwest side of the Metro east of Urbandale.  The storm also produced gusty winds in the 55 to 60 mph range reported at a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaverdale area report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea size hail was also reported from the spotter in addition to the strong winds.

A powerful upper level system was moving into south central Canada.  The upper level winds were increasing as a closed upper low settled into that area.  Warm air advection continued across Iowa through the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th.  The atmosphere was very unstable with CAPE values around 4000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1000 and 1500 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 800 J/kg range.  Strong shear was present, with an effective shear of 50 to 60 kts.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 15,000 feet.  Precipitable water increased to between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  There was a considerable amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere, between 100 and 175 J/kg.  That combined with an LCl of only 750 meters was a favorable environment for tornado development.  Thunderstorms formed rapidly in northwest and north central Iowa by the late afternoon and evening hours.  They spread and continued to move east through the night.  All modes of severe weather occurred with tornadoes occurring during the early and mid evening hours.  Two tornadoes were reported in the Stanhope area near the Webster and Hamilton County line.  A tornado touched down in Grundy County north of Conrad.  Winds caused damage in many areas across the north half of Iowa.  Hail was common with most of the storms in spite of the high freezing level.  Much of the hail an inch or less in diameter.  Larger hail of golf ball to tennis ball size fell in Butler and Bremer Counties.  A strong storm formed in Ringgold and Union Counties and dropped golf ball size hail.  The event produced very heavy rainfall in many locations.  The ground had become nearly saturated from previous rounds of storms during the week prior.  Flooding and flash flooding became a problem in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated to have fallen in the last 2 to 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong mid and upper level jet combined with high amounts of low level moisture and instability to create a volatile atmosphere across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa late in the night and into Monday morning. Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low-level jet and quickly became severe. Isolated to scattered supercells moved across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa through late morning before exiting into central Iowa.
