EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding over part of northwest Iowa on the afternoon of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall of 4.08 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south across Iowa during the night of the 14th into the morning of the 15th.  There was marginal instability...however a low level jet set up overnight and resulted in the development of thunderstorms across southern Iowa  Though most were not severe, one storm produced quarter size hail as it moved across Wayne and Appanoose Counties.
Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in the Lake Park and Spirit Lake area in Dickinson County in northwest Iowa during the predawn hours of August 21st.
Minor flooding occurred along the Mississippi River in McGregor (Clayton County). The flooding was a result of run off from heavy rains across Minnesota and Wisconsin and snow melt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Run off from heavy rains and snow melt pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in McGregor. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 16.53 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough of low pressure moved into the state during the afternoon of the 10th.  The atmosphere became slightly unstable with 500 to 1000 J/kg of CAPE available.  Downdraft CAPE was weak, around 500 J/kg, with around 300 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  Effective shear was around 40 kts and the freezing level was relatively low around 11,000 feet.  A scattered line of thunderstorms formed during the afternoon.  A few of them became severe and produced quarter size hail in Adair and Taylor Counties.

A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds gusts to be 60 to 70 mph with small branches coming down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted into northern Iowa during the day on April 12, 2014 and dragged cold front across eastern Iowa. This lead to the development of a line of thunderstorms that moved across eastern Iowa with hail up to the size of nickels and damaging winds mainly along and to the north of Interstate 80. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grain bins were destroyed on a farm.  The time of the event was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm airmass moved into Iowa on the 11th and 12th of April.  By the afternoon of the 12th, temperatures reached the 80s across a large part of Iowa with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 50s to around 60.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon and tracked southeast across the state during the evening and overnight hours.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE values increasing to 2000 to 2500 J/kg by late afternoon.   Downdraft CAPE rose to between 800 and 1200 J/kg, while CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 400 to 500 J/kg range  The freezing level was around 12,000 ft near the storms inception point.  Precipitable water values increased to just over an inch by evening.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 40 to 60 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms were initially capped off during much of the afternoon with only scattered storms forming along the front. The cap was broken by late afternoon into the evening. A line of strong to severe storms formed.  The primary mode of severe weather was hail with most storms producing at least some hail.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  A few of the storms produced larger hail, with 2.5 inch diameter hail reported in Story County, and 2 inch diameter hail in Butler and Bremer Counties.  After sunset, the storms began to produce severe winds with several gusts to around 60 MPH reported over the southeast third of the state.  As the winds increased, the hail became smaller.  Significant damage was done in Mahaska County by the strong winds.  Heavy straight-line winds caused several thousand dollars in damage to a farm.  The barn roof was damaged, a south wall partially gone, overhead door destroyed and wrapped around a trailer, skylights blown out, north wall section bowed out, and a 12' X 14' door was blown off. Three fruit trees were also blown down.  Roof damage was reported in Wayne County as very strong straight-line winds swept through the Promise City area.  In addition, a large shed was blown down by the winds south of Corydon.  During the night into the next morning the storms became multi-cellular and began to train over the state as the next shortwave advanced northeast toward the central U.S.  Locally heavy rain was reported with a 3.5 inch total in a 6-hour period reported in Clarke County of south central Iowa.  Twenty-four hour rainfall totals in Jasper and Poweshiek Counties were around 4 inches.  Two-day totals across much of the southeast half of Iowa were in the two to five inch range.

Heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms in late September caused a number of rivers in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa to go into flood. Much of the flooding was minor, but flooding along the Salt Creek and associated tributaries was more significant and in some locations exceeded 100 and 200 year return intervals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in late September led to river flooding into early October along the East Nishnabotna River. The river rose to flood stage in the afternoon on the 1st, crested in the evening, and fell below flood stage just after midnight on the 2nd. The flooding was minor and confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of very warm air moved into the state on the afternoon of the 6th with high temperatures reaching well into the 90s over the west half of the state.  Dewpoint readings rose to the low 60s.  The atmosphere became unstable, but was capped through much of the day.  By the evening, a cold front began to push into the northwest part of the state.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front and became severe quite quickly.  There was around 3000 J/kg of elevated CAPE available, though the shear was weak with 20 to 30 kts of effective shear.  The moisture was relatively limited.  Though there was 1.25 inches of precipitable water in the sounding, the low levels were quite dry.  Downdraft CAPE was high, with 1200 to 1700 J/kg available and the freezing level was quite high for early May, around 13,000 feet.  The available CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 300 and 600 J/kg.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the evening and produced hail and high winds.  Golf ball to two inch diameter hail fell in Calhoun and Sac Counties.  Winds gusts to nearly 65 MPH were observed on the back side of the storms in Carroll County in Carroll.  As the thunderstorms weakened and collapsed, they produced high winds.  An estimated wind gust of 70 MPH tore out a 26 x 48 inch tunnel and a twisted it and a play set was blown over north of Lytton in Calhoun County.  The thunderstorms continued to collapse and head northeast.  Winds gusted to 77 MPH at the Algona Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 26 x 48 tunnel was ripped from the ground and twisted.  A playset was blown over.  Winds were estimated to be 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over the central U.S. on the 3rd.  A strong short wave moved out of the Rockies onto the Plains during the day.  Thunderstorms formed early in Nebraska and South Dakota.  The storms spread and developed southeast into Iowa during the afternoon and evening.  The airmass became unstable with CAPE values rising to about 2000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE rose to to around 1000 J/kg, with about 600 J/kg of CAPE available in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet.  The atmosphere was highly sheared with 50 to 80 kts of shear available.  A strong 70 to 80 kt mid level jet moved into the area as the low level jet increased to around 60 kts by evening.  Precipitable water values were around 2 inches, which is more than three sigma above seasonal normals.  The LCL level was fairly low, around 1000 meters. Thunderstorms that formed to the west quickly became an upscale MCC as it continue southeast.  The strongest storms formed along the baroclinic zone of the warm front.  Windspread severe weather occurred, but was limited to the far southwest part of the CWA.  Hail and high wind was the mode of severe weather with hail up to baseball size and winds in excess of 80 MPH.  The winds caused considerable structural and tree damage across southwest Iowa.  Whole farm buildings were destroyed in Taylor and Ringgold Counties with winds of 70 to 95 MPH.  Very heavy rainfall also occurred with a band of three to six inches of rain.  Additional damage was done in Adams County when lightning struck a home in the town of Corning. The home was was a total loss from the fire. Spotty flash flooding was reported with some of it significant in Ringgold, Cass,  Adams, Adair, Taylor Wayne, and Decatur Counties.  Flood damage was widespread in Decatur County with the county engineer reporting 25 culverts damage, 12 bridges damaged and water over the road in 40 other locations, up to 2-3' of water at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were reported covered by water and had to be closed until water receded in southern Adair County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and muggy conditions were found across the region May 20 as a cold front tracked east across Iowa.  A strong line of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front and impacted areas mainly between Highway 20 and Highway 30 in Iowa as well as Carroll and Jo Daviess Counties in Illinois.  Very large hail fell with the stronger storms with hail sizes as large as golf balls over Dubuque, Jackson, and Jones Counties in Iowa.  Torrential rains were also common with these storms.  Elsewhere, dry and warm conditions were found. High temperatures were in the mid to upper 80s.

A stationary front was located south of Iowa as a series of shortwaves moved southeast across the state.  Moisture pooled with warm cloud depths reaching 4400 meters.  The precipitable water values increased to above two inches across a large part of Iowa.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms formed with training echoes producing heavy rainfall.  The highest unofficial rainfall was in the Audubon area with 6.2 inches.  There were numerous reports in the 2 to 3 inch range.  There was no flooding due the the antecedent conditions being very dry. Many of the areas had received little rainfall during the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.50 inches was measured in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 2.35 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture moved into southwest and west central Iowa during the early morning hours of the 28th.  The 850 mb winds increased to 40 to 50 kts to the south of the area, with winds of 20 to 25 kts over southwest Iowa.  Moisture pooled with precipitable water values increasing to 2.1 to 2.25 inches over the areas.  There was mechanical lift in place with a warm front located east-west to the south of the state.  Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measurement over the past 24 hours was 2.21 inches.

Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located south of Iowa as a series of shortwaves moved southeast across the state.  Moisture pooled with warm cloud depths reaching 4400 meters.  The precipitable water values increased to above two inches across a large part of Iowa.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms formed with training echoes producing heavy rainfall.  The highest unofficial rainfall was in the Audubon area with 6.2 inches.  There were numerous reports in the 2 to 3 inch range.  There was no flooding due the the antecedent conditions being very dry. Many of the areas had received little rainfall during the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.11 inches was measured in the past 22 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 3.48 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level disturbance moved slowly across southern Minnesota as a weak cold front entered northwest Iowa.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with about 2500 J/kg of available CAPE.  Zero to 3 km CAPE was near 100 J/kg.  Surface temperatures were in the mid 80s with dewpoints in the low to mid 70s.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  One of the storms on the southwest part of the line produced a funnel cloud northwest of Armstrong in Emmet County.  The storms continued on and produced a tornado east of Dolliver in Emmet County.  The tornado was on the ground for less than a minute before dissipating.  The tornado touched down in a farm field and resulted in only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emmet County Emergency Manager confirms a brief touchdown in a field about 2 miles north of the intersection of county highway N52 and state highway 9. The tornado was on the ground for about 2 minutes before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed on the leading edge of a QLCS and damaged mostly trees and crops along its path. One small open farm structure was destroyed. A distinct path was noted through corn fields.  Track also found and extended into Greene county using high resolution satellite imagery.

A warm front was positioned west-northwest to east-southeast to the south of Iowa.  An upper level disturbance moved into the area as the low level jet increased during the early morning hours of the 20th.  During the pre-dawn hours, the low level jet increased to 35 to 40 kts.  The airmass was unstable with about 2000 J/kg of elevated CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was near 600 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed north of the warm front.  A few of them became severe and produced large hail.  Quarter to golf ball size hail fell in Dallas and Polk Counties.  The largest hail fell in De Soto in Dallas County where golf ball size hail was reported.  A car was struck by lightning as a man was driving to work on Iowa Highway 5.  The man was not injured, but about $15,000 in damage occurred to the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, humid, and unstable airmass was in place across Iowa.  Temperatures were in the mid 80s to around 90, with dewpoint temperatures in the mid to upper 70s.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with around 5000 J/kg of CAPE available and an LCL around 1000 meters.  A warm front was located east-west across Iowa.  Thunderstorms formed along the front during the late afternoon.  There were numerous reports of funnel clouds in Boone and Webster Counties from the storms, with no other severe weather reported.

Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miso-vortex was generally weak but had a brief period of more intense circulation along its path.  Damage was found and documented using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.3 inches in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported by a driver driving north on P56 from C48 to C44 from around 2226 CST to 2233.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall 4.67 inches was received via CoCoRaHS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This small but potent tornado formed just over 3 miles north of Dayton and tracked nearly due east, destroying one hog confinement building and leaving a pronounced trail through multiple corn fields. The tornado weakened midway into its track, but still left a path 20 yards wide through several other corn fields before doing some minor tree damage at the end of its track.  Tornado was surveyed but track lengthened after high resolution satellite imagery showed additional damage and path length.

One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County. Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.

This tornado was found and rated using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds toppled trees at the corner of Orchard Drive and Laurinda in Cedar Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall over the past 24 hours was 4.40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water over 210th st from 2 southwest of Murray into Union County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary became stationary south of Iowa.  The surface front was over central Missouri, however the front at 850 mb was considerably farther north.  As the nocturnal low level jet formed, thunderstorms developed in southwest Iowa.  The airmass was unstable with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of elevated CAPE to the south of Iowa.  The storms produced rainfall in the one to two inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 2.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary approached Iowa from the northwest as an upper level low pressure area reached the western border during the afternoon.  The airmass became unstable with about 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  Shear was around 40 kts by late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose into the low to mid 80s with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to low 70s.  The downdraft CAPE was quite high over northeast Iowa around 1000 J/kg to just above.  Thunderstorms formed in an arching line ahead of the approaching upper low.  Though most of the storms were not severe, one became severe in Bremer County with a wind gust of 65 MPH in the Plainfield area.  Several trees were blown down and small hail also covered the ground.  Thunderstorms continued to develop in the warm air advection zone.  High winds damaged cars, a house, and trees in the Lake Mills area of Winnebago County.  A funnel cloud was observed northwest of Fonda in Pocahontas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down along with small hail covering the ground.  Wind gusts of 65 MPH were recorded at an RWIS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture moved into southwest and west central Iowa during the early morning hours of the 28th.  The 850 mb winds increased to 40 to 50 kts to the south of the area, with winds of 20 to 25 kts over southwest Iowa.  Moisture pooled with precipitable water values increasing to 2.1 to 2.25 inches over the areas.  There was mechanical lift in place with a warm front located east-west to the south of the state.  Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measurement over the past 24 hours was 1.18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall over the past 24 hours was 3.35 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 1.96 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was positioned west-northwest to east-southeast to the south of Iowa.  An upper level disturbance moved into the area as the low level jet increased during the early morning hours of the 20th.  During the pre-dawn hours, the low level jet increased to 35 to 40 kts.  The airmass was unstable with about 2000 J/kg of elevated CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was near 600 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed north of the warm front.  A few of them became severe and produced large hail.  Quarter to golf ball size hail fell in Dallas and Polk Counties.  The largest hail fell in De Soto in Dallas County where golf ball size hail was reported.  A car was struck by lightning as a man was driving to work on Iowa Highway 5.  The man was not injured, but about $15,000 in damage occurred to the car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was struck by lightning as the occupant was driving to work.

A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miso-vortex in northern Cerro Gordo county produced crop damage.  Damage was found and documented using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture moved into southwest and west central Iowa during the early morning hours of the 28th.  The 850 mb winds increased to 40 to 50 kts to the south of the area, with winds of 20 to 25 kts over southwest Iowa.  Moisture pooled with precipitable water values increasing to 2.1 to 2.25 inches over the areas.  There was mechanical lift in place with a warm front located east-west to the south of the state.  Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measurement over the past 24 hours was 1.67 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall since 1800 CST was 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.0 inches in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a gravel road just north of Belmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was positioned west-northwest to east-southeast to the south of Iowa.  An upper level disturbance moved into the area as the low level jet increased during the early morning hours of the 20th.  During the pre-dawn hours, the low level jet increased to 35 to 40 kts.  The airmass was unstable with about 2000 J/kg of elevated CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was near 600 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed north of the warm front.  A few of them became severe and produced large hail.  Quarter to golf ball size hail fell in Dallas and Polk Counties.  The largest hail fell in De Soto in Dallas County where golf ball size hail was reported.  A car was struck by lightning as a man was driving to work on Iowa Highway 5.  The man was not injured, but about $15,000 in damage occurred to the car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail reported by a KCWI Television viewer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, humid, and unstable airmass was in place across Iowa.  Temperatures were in the mid 80s to around 90, with dewpoint temperatures in the mid to upper 70s.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with around 5000 J/kg of CAPE available and an LCL around 1000 meters.  A warm front was located east-west across Iowa.  Thunderstorms formed along the front during the late afternoon.  There were numerous reports of funnel clouds in Boone and Webster Counties from the storms, with no other severe weather reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rope funnel cloud was observed.

One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short lived and narrow tornado remained in rural areas of western Hardin county producing only crop damage.  The tornado was found and rated using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture moved into southwest and west central Iowa during the early morning hours of the 28th.  The 850 mb winds increased to 40 to 50 kts to the south of the area, with winds of 20 to 25 kts over southwest Iowa.  Moisture pooled with precipitable water values increasing to 2.1 to 2.25 inches over the areas.  There was mechanical lift in place with a warm front located east-west to the south of the state.  Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measurement over the past 24 hours was 2.16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa. Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County. Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the previous 24 hours was 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along highway 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.54 inches in the past 6 hours.

A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 3.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 3.57 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary approached Iowa from the northwest as an upper level low pressure area reached the western border during the afternoon.  The airmass became unstable with about 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  Shear was around 40 kts by late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose into the low to mid 80s with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to low 70s.  The downdraft CAPE was quite high over northeast Iowa around 1000 J/kg to just above.  Thunderstorms formed in an arching line ahead of the approaching upper low.  Though most of the storms were not severe, one became severe in Bremer County with a wind gust of 65 MPH in the Plainfield area.  Several trees were blown down and small hail also covered the ground.  Thunderstorms continued to develop in the warm air advection zone.  High winds damaged cars, a house, and trees in the Lake Mills area of Winnebago County.  A funnel cloud was observed northwest of Fonda in Pocahontas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KIMT TV reported damages to cars, a house, and to trees by high winds from a thunderstorm.

An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past hour and 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 2.83 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall over the past 24 hours was 4.67 inches, four of which fell since midnight.

The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 2.42 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was positioned to the south of Iowa.  During the night of the 20th and early morning of the 21st, the low level jet increased to 50 kts to the southwest of the state.  The winds impinged on the warm front and set off thunderstorms on the edge of the mid level cap.  Most of the severe weather remained to the north of Iowa.  One of the storms moved into the northwest corner of the DMX CWA and produced a wind gust of 63 MPH at the Estherville Airport.

An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report that southwest 9th and Luster Lane had two stalled cars with water 6 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail occurred.  Small tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, humid, and unstable airmass was in place across Iowa.  Temperatures were in the mid 80s to around 90, with dewpoint temperatures in the mid to upper 70s.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with around 5000 J/kg of CAPE available and an LCL around 1000 meters.  A warm front was located east-west across Iowa.  Thunderstorms formed along the front during the late afternoon.  There were numerous reports of funnel clouds in Boone and Webster Counties from the storms, with no other severe weather reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of funnel clouds across northern Boone County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall over the past 24 hours was 4.40 inches, 4.10 of which fell last night.

Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.35 inches in the past 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches. A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast. Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 3.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A healthy tree 24 inches in diameter was snapped near the base by strong winds.

A frontal boundary became stationary south of Iowa.  The surface front was over central Missouri, however the front at 850 mb was considerably farther north.  As the nocturnal low level jet formed, thunderstorms developed in southwest Iowa.  The airmass was unstable with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of elevated CAPE to the south of Iowa.  The storms produced rainfall in the one to two inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of rainfall was measured at the 0600 CST observation time with another 0.25 inches since 0600 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located south of Iowa as a series of shortwaves moved southeast across the state.  Moisture pooled with warm cloud depths reaching 4400 meters.  The precipitable water values increased to above two inches across a large part of Iowa.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms formed with training echoes producing heavy rainfall.  The highest unofficial rainfall was in the Audubon area with 6.2 inches.  There were numerous reports in the 2 to 3 inch range.  There was no flooding due the the antecedent conditions being very dry. Many of the areas had received little rainfall during the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.50 inches was measured in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 2.55 inches.

Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 2.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall 3.25 inches was relayed by KCCI Television.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three very distinct vortex paths rotating around a common center.  These tracks left very well defined paths in crops across eastern Crawford county.  Several small miso-vortices were also noted nearby.  This tornado was found and rated using high resolution satellite imagery.

The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 1.55 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place across Iowa with afternoon highs reaching well into the 80s, and dewpoint readings in the low to mid 70s.  CAPE rose to 2000 to 3000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 to 1000 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 900 J/kg range.  Precipitable water increased to around 1.75 inches.  The freezing level was quite high at around 15,000 feet.  Fairly strong shear was present with 40 to 50 kts of effective shear by late afternoon.  A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front by late afternoon and moved east across the state through the evening hours.  Hail with the storms was generally quite small.  One inch diameter hail fell in Crawford County in Dow City.  The primary severe events from this system were high winds that occurred as the MCS progressed across the state.  Several locations reported gusts of 60 MPH or higher.  An 80 MPH gust was reported in Guthrie County at Yale and Bayard.  A 71 MPH wind gust occurred in Marion County.  Heavy rainfall was also of significance with flash flooding in Crawford County.  This was caused by a dam failure which flooded Highway 39.  Three inches of rain fell in 50 minutes in Dallas County.  There were numerous reports of around 2 inches of rain falling in Boone and Polk Counties.  Water ponding and low-land flooding was common.  One person was struck by lightning at the Denison Airport.  The person received minor injuries. Funnel clouds were sighted in both Crawford and Boone Counties.  Additional heavy rainfall events are included on 01 September 2014 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inch tree limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs were blown down by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from north central Kansas on the evening of the 15th, to north central Missouri by the morning of the 16th.  A warm front extended east from the low and lifted north slowly through the night.  A strong low level jet became established ahead of the low through the night with winds of 50 kts.  Precipitable water values increased to more than two inches over southern Iowa.  An MCS developed north of the warm front and tracked across the southern third of the state.  Widespread rainfall of two to four inches occurred with spotty rainfall around five inches.  The heaviest rainfall occurred in the Bedford area with official reports near five inches and unofficial reports of 7.5 inches about four miles southeast of Bedford.  There was also a report of 6.5 inches in Wayne County at Promise City.  Flooding was relatively minor due to relatively dry antecedent conditions and crop maturity.  Local rivers had minor flooding in the Davis City area of Taylor County, and in the Promise City area of Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall 4.42 inches was received via CoCoRaHS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located south of Iowa as a series of shortwaves moved southeast across the state.  Moisture pooled with warm cloud depths reaching 4400 meters.  The precipitable water values increased to above two inches across a large part of Iowa.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms formed with training echoes producing heavy rainfall.  The highest unofficial rainfall was in the Audubon area with 6.2 inches.  There were numerous reports in the 2 to 3 inch range.  There was no flooding due the the antecedent conditions being very dry. Many of the areas had received little rainfall during the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.10 inches was measured at the NWS office in Johnston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place over Iowa with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with CAPE around 2000 J/kg and shear around 30 kts.  A synoptic warm frontal boundary was located over northern Iowa, however a weak outflow boundary that remained from prior convection remained just south of the state.  As the low level jet increased in the evening, thunderstorms formed over southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms became a slow moving MCS as it drifted northeast.  They produced heavy rainfall with two to four inches common from southwest into central Iowa.  The heaviest rainfall report was in Cass County at Massena with 4.9 inches.  Some local flooding occurred, however there were no reports of either significant flooding or of severe weather with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the past 24 hours was 3.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon of the 24th through the evening hours.  The airmass was very moist and unstable with dewpoint values in the mid 70s over a large part of the state.  CAPE during the evening was around 3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was higher than it had been in recent days with around 40 kts available.  The downdraft CAPE was near 1000 J/kg with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 800 J/kg.  Deep moisture was present with precipitable water near 1.7 inches along the front.  Thunderstorms formed in a line along the front during the evening.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and hail around one half inch in diameter.  A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail north and west of Fort Dodge in Webster County.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in the Dakota City area of Humboldt County.  Wind damage occurred in Wright and Hancock Counties as high winds toppled trees.  After midnight, a scattered line of thunderstorms formed south of the main line.  One of the storms produced high winds and toppled trees in Cedar Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter sized hail at their residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture moved into southwest and west central Iowa during the early morning hours of the 28th.  The 850 mb winds increased to 40 to 50 kts to the south of the area, with winds of 20 to 25 kts over southwest Iowa.  Moisture pooled with precipitable water values increasing to 2.1 to 2.25 inches over the areas.  There was mechanical lift in place with a warm front located east-west to the south of the state.  Widespread rainfall of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Flooding was minor, however ditches and small streams filled to capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measurement over the past 24 hours was 1.74 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located south of Iowa as a series of shortwaves moved southeast across the state.  Moisture pooled with warm cloud depths reaching 4400 meters.  The precipitable water values increased to above two inches across a large part of Iowa.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms formed with training echoes producing heavy rainfall.  The highest unofficial rainfall was in the Audubon area with 6.2 inches.  There were numerous reports in the 2 to 3 inch range.  There was no flooding due the the antecedent conditions being very dry. Many of the areas had received little rainfall during the previous month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.76 inches was reported in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place with warm cloud depths near 4.5 km.  Precipitable water values were in the 2.2 to 2.4 inch range over a large part of Iowa.  A thunderstorm complex formed over eastern Nebraska during the evening of the 22nd.  The complex grew upscale into an MCC as it moved into Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over southern Iowa.  A large area of two to four inch rainfall occurred over southern Iowa.  There were spotty reports near 5 inches.  The rain fell in areas that had received significant rainfall during the previous two days.  Flash flooding was reported in the Murray area of Clarke County.  Water was reported over U.S. Highway 69 south of Osceola.  Water of 12 to 18 inches in depth was reported in the Murray area with numerous roads closed, including Highway R15.  A line of strong storms moved through the Mason City area and dropped 1.5 inches of rain in about a half hour.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in town and the roof of a Walmart store collapsed from the weight of the water.  Once the flash flooding ended, generally flooding continued through the afternoon and evening.  The most significant was in Clarke County where flood waters nearly closed Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the past 24 hours was 2.62 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 9:45 p.m on 8/31 in Grundy County, Iowa just south of County HWY D25 on J Ave our farm lost 3/4 of it's machine shed roof on the SE side.  Part of the roof landed straight east short of 300 ft to 400 ft down driveway and the other part west in front of the shed but on the NE side.  More steel was to the NE about 300 ft to 400 ft and just missed our house.  The steel bent over the cable dish.  There was insulation splattered all over the garage and house to the NE of where roof went off and both garage doors were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Lime Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down in Colwell. The power was also out and was thought to be from lightning hitting a transformer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of August 31st across northeast Iowa ahead of northward moving warm front. These storms produced high winds that blew down trees in Cresco (Howard County) and produced a 61 mph wind gust in Oelwein (Fayette County). In addition, 3, short-lived, EF-0 tornadoes occurred, that were part of a larger outbreak of tornadoes across central Iowa. Refer to WFO DMX for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph occurred south of Rockford.
